articles.title: Two Kinds of Bowel Growths Can Make Cancer Risk Much Higher
articles.summary: Scientists found that two types of growths in the bowel, called adenomas and serrated polyps, can increase the chance of getting bowel cancer. Bowel cancer is a serious disease that affects many people. These growths are usually not harmful, but when both types are found together in a person, the risk of cancer becomes five times higher. The study looked at over 8,400 records from colonoscopy tests, which are used to check the bowel. The researchers say that catching these growths early with regular screening is very important, especially for people over 45 or with family history. This helps doctors remove the growths before they turn into cancer.

articles.title: How Bad Colds and COVID Can Affect Lungs Later
articles.summary: This article talks about how getting very sick with COVID-19 or the flu might make it easier for lung cancer to grow later on. Scientists from UVA Health found that when people have severe lung infections, it can change the cells in their lungs in a way that helps cancer develop faster. They studied mice and people and saw that those with bad infections were more likely to get lung cancer years later. The good news is that getting vaccinated can help prevent these harmful changes. Doctors now suggest watching patients who recover from severe COVID or flu closely so they can catch any cancer early when it's easier to treat. This research helps us understand why taking care of our lungs is important, especially after a serious illness.

articles.title: Medieval Burials: Sick People Were Treated Like Everyone Else
articles.summary: In medieval Denmark, people were often buried based on how much money they had, with richer people getting graves closer to the church. Scientists studied skeletons from old cemeteries to see if people with diseases like leprosy and tuberculosis were treated differently after they died. They looked at 939 skeletons from five cemeteries in Denmark, some in towns and some in the countryside. Leprosy made people look different with visible marks, while tuberculosis was less obvious. Surprisingly, the study found that people with these diseases were often buried in the same good spots as healthy people, not pushed away. This shows that medieval communities sometimes cared for sick people just like their neighbors, challenging old ideas that they were always treated badly. The research helps us understand how people in the past thought about illness and fairness.

articles.title: A Super Hard Test for Smart Computers
articles.summary: Scientists made a very hard test called Humanity's Last Exam to check how smart computers are. Before, computers did well on old tests, so scientists needed a new one. This test has 2,500 questions about math, science, history, and other tricky topics. Experts from around the world wrote the questions to make sure they are fair and have one right answer. They tested it on computers like GPT-4o and Claude, and most computers got low scores, showing the test is tough. The goal is to find what computers still can't do well, so people know their limits. A professor from Texas A&M helped make the test and said it's important to measure computer smarts correctly.

articles.title: NASA's Exciting Moon Trip in April
articles.summary: NASA is planning to send astronauts on a special mission called Artemis II in early April. This mission will fly around the Moon, which hasn't happened with people for over 50 years. The rocket had a small leak, so it was fixed in Florida. Now, NASA is moving it back to the launchpad on March 19, and the launch could be on April 1. Four astronauts from the US and Canada will ride in the Orion spacecraft on the big Space Launch System rocket. They will travel for 10 days, going around the far side of the Moon that we never see from Earth. NASA leaders talk about being careful with risks to make sure everything goes well. They are confident and ready for this exciting space adventure.

articles.title: Ancient Peru's Colorful Bird Trade
articles.summary: Long ago, before the famous Inca Empire, a kingdom called Ychsma in Peru loved colorful parrots. They traded to get live parrots from faraway rainforests across the Andes mountains. Scientists studied old feathers from headdresses found in tombs. The feathers were bright blue, yellow, and green. Tests showed the parrots were born in the wild but kept as pets in Peru, eating local food. This means people captured them hundreds of miles away and brought them over big mountains. Parrot feathers were very important for showing status and power. They were used in headdresses and clothes. This trade helped connect different places in South America. The Ychsma kingdom had a smart trade network without a big empire telling them what to do.
